steps.explanation: There is no separate 'sec button' in the exact-value work; we always convert $\sec$ back to a cosine and evaluate that instead.
Substituting $\theta=60^\circ$ turns the problem into finding one over $\cos 60^\circ$, a standard exact value.

steps.description: Recall the exact value of cos 60
steps.explanation: The angle $60^\circ$ comes from the half of an equilateral triangle, one of the two standard triangles, giving $\cos 60^\circ=\tfrac12$.
Replacing $\cos 60^\circ$ by $\tfrac12$ leaves a single division: one divided by a half.

steps.description: Simplify the reciprocal of a fraction
steps.explanation: Dividing by a fraction means multiplying by its reciprocal, so dividing by $\tfrac12$ is the same as multiplying by $2$.
Since a cosine never exceeds $1$ in size, its reciprocal can never be smaller than $1$ in size. A value of $2$ is comfortably consistent with this.
This is an exact result, not a rounded decimal, which is exactly what the question asks for.
